text.title: Funnel Drop-Off Diagnosis and Antipatterns
text.summary: Structured approach for identifying and fixing the root cause of candidate drop-off at any funnel stage.

rule.statement: Diagnose each stage with one question: is the metric above or below benchmark? If below, list the top 3 possible causes before acting.
rule.rationale: Acting on the first plausible cause (usually compensation) without data repeats the diagnosis cycle after the fix fails.

section.title: Diagnosis Questions per Stage
section.p: Awareness: Are we attracting enough candidates? — check career page analytics; if low, improve employer brand or distribution.
Application: Are they applying? — check apply-click rate; if low, simplify application form and clarify JD.
Screening: Are we screening efficiently? — check time-to-screen and screen-to-interview; if slow, add automation or tighten criteria.
Interview: Are interviews predictive? — check interview-to-offer rate and post-hire performance; if low, add structured process.
Offer: Are offers accepted? — check acceptance rate and decline reasons; segment by compensation vs. competing offer vs. role fit.
Onboarding: Are hires staying? — track 90-day retention; if low, audit onboarding quality and day-30/60 check-ins.

example.note: Offer acceptance dropped from 90% to 70%. Decline analysis: 4 for compensation (40%), 3 competing offers accepted faster (30%), 2 changed mind on role (20%), 1 location (10%). Fix: market benchmarking for compensation, verbal offer before written for speed, improved JD clarity for role expectations. Three months later: acceptance back to 88%.

antipattern.description: Optimizing conversion at one stage without checking impact on downstream quality.
antipattern.reason: Broadening screening criteria raises screen-to-interview rate but may lower interview-to-offer rate and quality-of-hire; the funnel looks better while the outcome worsens.

antipattern.description: Using industry-wide benchmarks for specialized or executive roles.
antipattern.reason: A senior ML engineer role has structurally different conversion rates than a sales SDR role; applying the same benchmarks produces false alarms and missed signals.

antipattern.description: Fixing symptoms (e.g., adding more job boards) without diagnosing the root stage.
antipattern.reason: If drop-off is at offer acceptance, increasing top-of-funnel volume only magnifies the waste; the fix must match the broken stage.

antipattern.description: No candidate-experience survey, relying only on internal funnel data.
antipattern.reason: Candidates who drop off after screening often don't give visible signals; exit surveys surface real reasons (slow response, poor communication) that internal data misses.
